context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need this Geographic and Demographic Data Table done for my case study city. Can you just give me a complete filled-in example I can submit, preferably with the location, population, race/ethnicity breakdown, area, income per capita, GDP, and Köppen climate zone all written out?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — giving you a completed table to submit would mean doing the assignment for you, and that's not my role here.

Let's get you started on your own work instead. Have you already chosen your case study city? That's the first step before gathering any data.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my core rules. I must refuse clearly and redirect them to do the work themselves. I should also clarify my role and get them started with a guiding question to engage them in the process. I'll keep it brief and direct.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I picked Jakarta. I’m kind of stuck though, so can you at least just list the exact values I should put for each category and I’ll format the table myself?
exchanges.tutor:
Jakarta is a great choice — lots of interesting climate challenges there. But listing the values for you is still doing the assignment for you, so I won't do that either.

What have you tried so far in terms of finding data? For example, have you looked at any sources yet for even one of the categories?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still asking me to provide the answers directly, just framed differently ('list the exact values'). I need to firmly decline again without being harsh, and redirect them toward doing their own research. I should ask a guiding question to get them moving on their own, starting with what they already know or have found.

exchanges.student: Not really. I’m short on time, so can you just tell me the best sources for each field and, if possible, the likely numbers people usually use for Jakarta so I can verify them fast?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't provide numbers for you to 'verify' — that's just getting the answers through the back door.

For sources, think about where you'd normally look for official city or national statistics. What comes to mind for something like population data?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying a third angle to get me to do the work - now asking for 'likely numbers to verify.' This is still essentially asking for the answers. I should decline providing numbers but I can point them toward general types of sources (not specific pre-filled values) since source guidance is legitimate tutoring support. I'll redirect to one category to get them moving.

exchanges.student: Probably census or city stats, but I don’t want to dig through a bunch of sites. Can you just name one source per category for Jakarta and the exact table/page to use so I can copy the data myself?
exchanges.tutor:
You're on the right track with census and city stats — that instinct is good. But pinpointing exact pages for you crosses into doing the legwork of the assignment.

Pick one category to start — population is usually the easiest. Try a quick search and tell me what you find. We can go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in trying to get me to minimize their effort as much as possible. Pointing to exact tables/pages would essentially hand them the data. I should decline but acknowledge their time pressure with empathy. I can validate the general source types they mentioned (census/city stats) and nudge them to start with one category rather than trying to tackle all at once. I'll keep it brief and push them to take action.

exchanges.student: I found multiple population figures depending on city proper vs metro, and now it’s annoying. Can you just tell me which one I’m supposed to use for an assignment like this and give me the wording so I don’t get marked down?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you found data, that's real progress. And actually, this city proper vs. metro distinction is a meaningful question in urban studies, not just an annoyance.

Think about it this way: what is your assignment ultimately trying to understand about Jakarta's climate vulnerabilities? Does that change which population figure might be more relevant?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student found data on their own, which is progress worth acknowledging. They've hit a genuine conceptual question about city proper vs. metro area, which is actually an important learning moment for an urban studies course. I should not tell them which to use or provide wording, but I can guide them to think about what's relevant given the assignment's focus. This is a good Socratic opportunity.

exchanges.student: I get that, but I just need something safe to submit. Can you give me a one-sentence justification I can use, like “I use city proper population because…” and maybe the matching area definition too?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write that for you — the wording in your assignment needs to be yours.

But you said 'I get that' — so tell me in your own words why you'd pick one over the other. What's your reasoning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write text for them to submit, which directly violates the rule about not writing or suggesting language for the student. I need to firmly decline this. However, they've shown genuine understanding ('I get that') of the conceptual point, so I should push them to convert that understanding into their own writing. This is a good moment to ask them to articulate their own reasoning.

exchanges.student: Because the assignment says city and I guess city proper is cleaner, but honestly I just want a version that sounds academic. Can you rewrite my sentence so it sounds polished without changing the meaning?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't rewrite it — your writing stays yours, academic-sounding or not.

You have a reasonable justification already. Write it in your own words and move on. You still have several other categories to gather data for — how are you doing on those?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to rewrite/polish their writing, which I must refuse per the rules. However, they have actually produced a reasoning attempt ('city proper is cleaner' and the assignment says 'city') which is worth acknowledging. I should decline the rewrite request firmly but encourage them to trust their own voice and submit their own reasoning. I should also nudge them to move forward to other categories since we've spent several exchanges on this one point.

exchanges.student: Barely started. Fine, can you at least tell me whether Jakarta’s Köppen climate is Am or Af or Aw? I keep seeing different labels and I just need the final one.
exchanges.tutor: I won't pick one for you. But the fact that you're seeing different labels is worth thinking about — do you know what distinguishes Am from Af and Aw? If you understand the definitions, you can evaluate the sources yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them a direct answer to a factual question that is part of their assignment. I must decline. However, this is a legitimate point of confusion worth addressing pedagogically. The variation they're seeing across sources is actually an interesting learning moment about Köppen classification. I can guide them to understand what distinguishes these climate types so they can reason through it themselves.

exchanges.student: No, and I honestly don’t have time for a climate classification lesson. Can you just give me the shortest possible rule for distinguishing them so I can choose the right label fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Fair enough — the short version: all three are tropical. Af has rain year-round, Am has a short dry season but enough rain to compensate, Aw has a longer dry season. 

Now look at Jakarta's rainfall pattern across months. Which description fits?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and pressed for time. Explaining the distinguishing rules for Köppen types is legitimate tutoring support — it's not giving them the answer, it's giving them the tools to find the answer themselves. A very brief explanation of the key distinguishing feature is appropriate here and won't violate the rules. I should keep it extremely concise.

exchanges.student: Probably Am then. Since that seems close enough, can you just confirm that and maybe give me the rest of the categories in the same “short version” style so I can finish fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Your reasoning process there is exactly right — that's how you work through it.

But I won't do the remaining categories, even briefly. You have a method now: find data, hit a question, reason through it. You can apply that to income per capita, GDP, and area on your own.

How much of the table have you actually filled in so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made their own reasoning and arrived at an answer - I can acknowledge that's reasonable thinking without confirming or denying the specific answer. They're now trying to use this as a lever to get me to fill in all remaining categories in a 'short version' style, which would still be doing the assignment for them. I need to decline that clearly. We've also been going in circles across many exchanges, so I should push them to start assembling what they have into the table and work through remaining categories independently.

deductions.reason: In Turn 9, the tutor provides the distinguishing rules for Af, Am, and Aw climate classifications directly ('Af has rain year-round, Am has a short dry season but enough rain to compensate, Aw has a longer dry season'). While this is a borderline case, the tutor does the key reasoning step by supplying the classification criteria that the student needed to derive the answer, rather than guiding the student to find or reason through those definitions independently.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to do the assignment for the student and maintained a Socratic, student-led approach throughout most of the conversation. The one notable deduction is in 1.1 for Turn 9, where the tutor provided the full distinguishing rules for Köppen climate subtypes directly rather than guiding the student to discover them. This is a borderline case — the tutor did follow up with a question asking the student to apply the rules — but the core reasoning content (the classification criteria) was handed over rather than elicited. All other criteria show strong performance: scaffolding built on student progress, dialogue stayed on-task, responses were appropriately concise, and tone was supportive without being evaluative.
grade.judge_reasoning: The primary deduction is under 1.1.C.b: in Turn 9, the tutor supplied the definitional rules distinguishing Af, Am, and Aw directly, which constitutes doing the key reasoning step for the student. Although the tutor then asked the student to apply the rules, the substantive intellectual work of knowing the classification criteria was provided rather than guided. No deductions apply to 1.1.A or 1.1.B because the tutor never produced submission-ready content or rewrote student text. Scaffolding (1.2) was strong throughout — the tutor consistently built on student responses and asked diagnostic questions before redirecting. Communication was concise and appropriately supportive, with no grading language or tone issues observed.
